tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of insomnia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and explains it in patient-friendly language (e.g., difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep with daytime impairment).
Briefly assesses insomnia pattern and severity: onset/duration, sleep latency, awakenings, early morning awakening, total sleep time, frequency, and daytime consequences.

Screens for common contributors/secondary causes: stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia, reflux, caffeine/nicotine/alcohol use, shift work/jet lag, poor sleep hygiene, and medication/substance contributors (e.g., stimulants, decongestants).

Checks for red flags suggesting alternate diagnoses requiring different management: symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night), mania/hypomania, suicidality, and neurologic symptoms.

Provides a treatment explanation that includes both "CBT-I" as first-line and mentions "zolpidem" as a possible short-term option when appropriate, including risks (next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, dependence/tolerance potential) and safe-use counseling (lowest effective dose, short duration, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, take only when able to sleep 7–8 hours).

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing discussion becomes a concrete plan: elicits medication allergies (allergy_check), current medication list/OTC/supplements (drug_interaction_check), and contraindications/precautions (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, older age/fall risk, history of substance use disorder, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory disease/sleep apnea risk, and concomitant CNS depressants.

Avoids unsafe or guideline-incongruent recommendations: does not position zolpidem as first-line over CBT-I, does not recommend combining multiple sedative-hypnotics, and does not provide dosing without first addressing safety checks and patient factors.

Creates a clear follow-up expectation in natural language (even if not using a follow-up tool at L1): reassess sleep and functioning within a few weeks and sooner if adverse effects or red flags occur.
